items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!".

rules_checked.reasoning: The item declares kind 'type' and describes a concrete struct/class `Greeter` with two methods returning fixed strings — this is genuinely a type declaration, not a function, protocol, or config; the declared kind matches what it actually is.

Target is Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift, inside the Greeter target defined in Package.swift (`.target(name: "Greeter")`). The existing GreeterModule.swift namespace marker in the same directory confirms this is the intended home for the feature's first type. Correct module placement.

This is a plain library target with no view/view-model layering in this walking-skeleton project; the logic is simple string formatting inside a single type, not domain logic leaking into a view or a view model reaching into data access. No altitude violation applies.

A new type is warranted: there is no existing Greeter type to extend (only a namespace-marker enum GreeterModule with no members), and the acceptance criteria require a coherent unit exposing two related greet() overloads — one small, single-responsibility type is the right grain, not over-fragmentation.
The item introduces a concrete type only, no protocol. No speculative abstraction is proposed, so this rule is satisfied trivially.

`Greeter` as a type name and `greet(name:)` / `greet()` as method names follow Swift API Design Guidelines: noun for the type, verb for the mutating/producing method, argument label `name` is clear at the call site, and overload without argument reads naturally as `greeter.greet()`.

The type is self-contained with no imports beyond the standard library implied; it introduces no dependency edges, forbidden imports, or reversed dependency direction — it sits alone in the Greeter target with no coupling concerns.
